title: Records and entities

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Records and entities; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Development Fundamentals; August 2023; section applicable to Records and entities
sources.claim: A record groups named fields that describe one conceptual entity, with each field chosen for a defined meaning, type, optionality, and validation rule.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A record groups named fields that describe one conceptual entity, with each field chosen for a defined meaning, type, optionality, and validation rule.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A student record groups id, display name, enrollment date, and active status while keeping calculated progress outside the editable identity fields. Your goal is to design a record representation and justify its fields and types. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Iteration; Values and types. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: A record groups named fields that describe one conceptual entity, with each field chosen for a defined meaning, type, optionality, and validation rule. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Records do not automatically enforce identity, uniqueness, normalization, privacy, or database relationships, and duplicated derived fields can become inconsistent. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Any values frequently used together should be copied into one record, including every derived and unrelated field.

includes: Group related named fields that describe one conceptual entity.
Design a record representation and justify its fields and types.
excludes: Records do not automatically enforce identity, uniqueness, normalization, privacy, or database relationships, and duplicated derived fields can become inconsistent.

outcomes: Design a record representation and justify its fields and types.

examples.title: Records and entities: direct case
examples.situation: A student record groups id, display name, enrollment date, and active status while keeping calculated progress outside the editable identity fields.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A student record groups id, display name, enrollment date, and active status while keeping calculated progress outside the editable identity fields.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Group cohesive facts about one entity, name their meanings and constraints, and derive redundant values rather than storing conflicting copies when possible.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Group cohesive facts about one entity, name their meanings and constraints, and derive redundant values rather than storing conflicting copies when possible.

examples.title: Records and entities: boundary case
examples.situation: A coordinate record names latitude and longitude with range constraints so callers do not confuse two unlabeled numeric positions.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A coordinate record names latitude and longitude with range constraints so callers do not confuse two unlabeled numeric positions.

Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Records do not automatically enforce identity, uniqueness, normalization, privacy, or database relationships, and duplicated derived fields can become inconsistent.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Any values frequently used together should be copied into one record, including every derived and unrelated field.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Design a record representation and justify its fields and types.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Any values frequently used together should be copied into one record, including every derived and unrelated field.
misconceptions.correction: Group cohesive facts about one entity, name their meanings and constraints, and derive redundant values rather than storing conflicting copies when possible.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Any values frequently used together should be copied into one record, including every derived and unrelated field.

analogy.example: An organized storeroom can model Records and entities: shelves preserve order, labeled bins group fields, membership lists avoid duplicates, and indexes locate values.
usefulFor: Comparing organization by order, name, membership, lookup, and update needs
limitations: Physical storage hides concrete complexity, equality, hashing, mutation, allocation, and concurrency guarantees.
Records do not automatically enforce identity, uniqueness, normalization, privacy, or database relationships, and duplicated derived fields can become inconsistent.

recap.summary: You should now be able to design a record representation and justify its fields and types. Keep this boundary in view: Records do not automatically enforce identity, uniqueness, normalization, privacy, or database relationships, and duplicated derived fields can become inconsistent.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Records and entities without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: Any values frequently used together should be copied into one record, including every derived and unrelated field.
Apply the model to this case from memory: A coordinate record names latitude and longitude with range constraints so callers do not confuse two unlabeled numeric positions.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Sets and maps”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
